narrative: As a white male applicant with an undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and an LSAT score of 33, your early academic record placed you in the 2nd decile for Year 1 grades and the 3rd decile for Year 3 at your school—below the typical 5th or 6th decile averages for admitted students who go on to succeed. Your family's upper-middle income background is solid, and planning to attend full-time aligns with the vast majority of successful bar takers. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam outcomes based on profiles like yours, estimated a 45% chance you'll fail the exam—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants with stronger projected pass odds to maintain their outcomes. Among all the factors considered, your undergraduate GPA had the strongest positive influence, pulling the prediction toward success because, while slightly below the 3.21 average, it still signaled decent foundational strength. Your LSAT score came in second, also helping by showing solid reasoning ability, even if under the 36.7 average. However, your Year 3 decile rank was the top factor pushing toward failure, as being in the lower third of your class raised concerns about sustaining performance under law school pressures. These three elements—undergrad GPA helping the most, followed by LSAT support, then Year 3 grades tipping against you—dominated the model's close-call decision. Compared to typical students who pass, your metrics are competitive in some spots but lag in class standing, which amplified the risk signal. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a stable background; it's a data-driven filter many schools use now. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it above average, strengthening later-year grades through targeted courses, or applying to programs with more flexible bar passage criteria. Building a standout personal statement highlighting resilience could also sway holistic reviews elsewhere. With focused improvements, your profile has room to shine in the next cycle.
